refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability of ≥0.97 (97%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the system is available for productive operation to total scheduled operational time, accounting for planned maintenance, unplanned downtime, and safety-driven shutdowns.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-004 is a key performance indicator (KPI) that directly impacts customer satisfaction and mission success. Refining it requires explicit definition of: (1) the measurement boundary (what constitutes 'available'), (2) the evaluation period (e.g., 30 days, 90 days, 1 year), (3) exclusions (planned maintenance windows, customer-initiated pauses), and (4) the role of safety-driven shutdowns. High safety criticality means availability must not be achieved by deferring safety actions; safety events must be logged separately.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous telemetry collection of system state transitions (operational, maintenance, fault, safety-hold). Automated calculation of availability ratio at end of evaluation period. Third-party audit of logs to confirm measurement methodology compliance with customer contract.
sub_frs: FR-AVAIL-001: The system shall detect and log all transitions to non-available states (faults, maintenance, safety holds) with timestamp and root cause.
FR-AVAIL-002: The system shall minimize unplanned downtime through predictive maintenance and fault isolation.
FR-AVAIL-003: The system shall provide real-time availability status visibility to Site Manager and Tele-operator.
FR-AVAIL-004: The system shall distinguish between safety-driven unavailability and performance-driven unavailability in reporting.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement redundant subsystems (dual motors, dual control channels, dual sensors) with automatic failover to mask single-point failures and reduce mean-time-to-repair (MTTR).
DP-B: Implement continuous health monitoring with predictive diagnostics and scheduled preventive maintenance intervals to reduce unplanned failures.
DP-C: Implement modular, hot-swappable component design with rapid replacement procedures to minimize repair time.
DP-D: Implement a combination of redundancy (DP-A), predictive health monitoring (DP-B), and modular design (DP-C) with integrated availability tracking and reporting.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Implement a multi-faceted availability strategy combining: (1) selective redundancy on critical safety-related subsystems (e.g., steering, braking, emergency stop), (2) continuous health monitoring with predictive fault detection and scheduled maintenance triggers, (3) modular component design enabling rapid field replacement, and (4) integrated telemetry-based availability tracking with real-time status reporting to stakeholders. This approach balances cost, complexity, and safety criticality.

potential_sources: Redundancy design (DP-A component) couples with Vehicle architecture—must ensure failover logic does not introduce new failure modes.
Predictive diagnostics (DP-B component) couples with Telemetry interface—must ensure sensor data quality and latency do not degrade prediction accuracy.
Modular design (DP-C component) couples with maintenance procedures and spare parts logistics—must ensure field replacement training and parts availability are in place.
Availability tracking (DP-D reporting component) couples with SafetyAlert interface—must ensure safety-driven holds are correctly classified and not masked in availability metrics.
coupling_check.notes: DP-D is a composite design parameter integrating multiple sub-strategies. Coupling is manageable if each sub-strategy is assigned to a distinct IM boundary with clear interface contracts. Risk: if redundancy and diagnostics are not coordinated, false positives in fault detection could trigger unnecessary failovers, reducing availability.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Customer evaluation period is ≥30 days; shorter periods may not provide statistically significant availability data.
ASSUMPTION: Planned maintenance windows are pre-scheduled and excluded from availability calculation.
ASSUMPTION: Telemetry infrastructure (sensors, network) is reliable enough to support continuous health monitoring; if telemetry itself is unreliable, predictive diagnostics will fail.
RISK: Redundancy adds cost and complexity; over-redundancy may reduce availability if failover logic is buggy.
RISK: Predictive diagnostics require historical failure data; early in product lifecycle, data may be sparse, reducing prediction accuracy.
RISK: Modular design may introduce additional failure modes at module interfaces (e.g., connector failures).
RISK: Safety-driven shutdowns (e.g., emergency stop activation) will reduce measured availability; if safety events are frequent, target may not be achievable without addressing root safety issues.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Clarify with customer: (1) Is the 0.97 target inclusive or exclusive of safety-driven shutdowns? (2) What is the agreed evaluation period? (3) Are there specific subsystems (e.g., steering, propulsion) that must have higher availability than others? (4) What is the acceptable cost/complexity trade-off for redundancy? Recommend a phased approach: Phase 1 (MVP) implements health monitoring and modular design; Phase 2 adds selective redundancy on highest-criticality subsystems.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution IM is the top-level system boundary encompassing all subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) and their integration. It owns the end-to-end availability measurement, reporting, and coordination logic. Specifically: (1) aggregation of health status from Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services, (2) calculation of system-level availability KPI, (3) real-time status reporting to stakeholders, (4) coordination of maintenance scheduling across subsystems.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Inbound telemetry stream from Vehicle and Safety Services subsystems, including health status, fault codes, sensor readings, and event logs. Used to populate availability tracking database and trigger predictive diagnostics.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional tasking interface with Site Manager and Tele-operator. Inbound: operational requests, maintenance scheduling directives. Outbound: real-time availability status, maintenance recommendations, fault alerts.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Outbound safety alerts to Safety Manager and emergency response systems. Triggered by safety-critical faults or anomalies detected during health monitoring. Includes classification of safety-driven unavailability events.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Underlying network infrastructure supporting all telemetry, tasking, and safety alert communications. NGHE Solution depends on Network Link reliability for real-time monitoring and reporting.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the natural top-level owner of availability KPI measurement and reporting. All sub-strategies (redundancy, health monitoring, modular design) are coordinated at this level. Minimal diffusion: only the actual implementation of redundancy logic (DP-A) and modular component design (DP-C) will be distributed to Vehicle IM; health monitoring (DP-B) and availability tracking (DP-D reporting) are contained here.

Vehicle IM owns the physical redundancy implementation (dual motors, dual sensors) and modular component design. However, Vehicle does not own the system-level availability calculation or reporting logic; that belongs to NGHE Solution. If availability tracking were assigned to Vehicle, it would create diffusion: Vehicle would need to aggregate data from Teleoperation and Safety Services, violating separation of concerns.

Teleoperation IM is responsible for remote operation and control, not system-level availability. Assigning availability KPI to Teleoperation would create high diffusion: Teleoperation would need to monitor Vehicle and Safety Services health, which is outside its functional scope. Not recommended.

Safety Services IM owns safety-critical monitoring and emergency response. It contributes to availability by detecting and reporting safety-driven shutdowns, but does not own the system-level availability KPI. Assigning the full availability requirement to Safety Services would create diffusion: Safety Services would need to track non-safety faults and maintenance events, which is outside its scope.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: NGHE Solution is the correct IM boundary for CR-NGHE-004 because: (1) Availability is a system-level KPI that requires aggregation of data from multiple subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services). (2) The measurement and reporting logic is inherently cross-cutting and cannot be localized to a single subsystem. (3) NGHE Solution already owns the integration and coordination of subsystems, making it the natural owner of system-level metrics. (4) Assigning availability to any subsystem would create diffusion and violate separation of concerns.
containment_risks: RISK: If NGHE Solution IM is not given explicit ownership of availability tracking infrastructure (database, calculation engine, reporting dashboard), the requirement may diffuse across multiple subsystems, leading to inconsistent measurement and reporting.

RISK: If the interface contracts between NGHE Solution and Vehicle/Teleoperation/Safety Services do not clearly specify what health data must be provided (via Telemetry interface), NGHE Solution may lack the data needed for accurate availability calculation.

RISK: If Network Link reliability is poor, NGHE Solution may not receive timely telemetry data, causing availability calculation to be inaccurate or delayed. This is a dependency risk, not a containment risk, but must be managed.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Explicitly define the NGHE Solution IM to include: (1) Availability Tracking Module (database and calculation engine), (2) Health Aggregation Module (collects telemetry from Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services), (3) Reporting Module (generates real-time status and KPI reports). Ensure that Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services IMs are contractually obligated to provide health telemetry via the Telemetry interface. Define the Telemetry interface schema to include: timestamp, subsystem ID, health status (operational/degraded/failed), fault code, and event type (fault/maintenance/safety-hold).

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (multi-faceted availability strategy) directly addresses CR-NGHE-004 (availability KPI). The strategy combines redundancy, health monitoring, modular design, and tracking—all of which are necessary to achieve and sustain ≥0.97 availability. The DP is not over-constrained and allows flexibility in implementation (e.g., phased rollout of redundancy). Coupling risks are identified and manageable.

diffusion_risks: Redundancy implementation (DP-A sub-component) will diffuse to Vehicle IM, which is acceptable because Vehicle owns the physical subsystems. However, failover logic must be clearly owned: either Vehicle owns it (if failover is local to Vehicle) or NGHE Solution owns it (if failover requires coordination across subsystems). Recommend: Vehicle owns local failover (e.g., motor failover); NGHE Solution owns system-level failover coordination.

Predictive diagnostics (DP-B sub-component) will require algorithms and data processing; ownership should be in NGHE Solution (which has access to aggregated telemetry) rather than Vehicle (which has only local sensor data). Risk: if diagnostics are implemented in Vehicle, they may lack context from other subsystems, reducing prediction accuracy.

Modular design (DP-C sub-component) will diffuse to Vehicle IM (physical component design) and potentially to Teleoperation IM (if remote operation requires modular software architecture). This is acceptable if module boundaries are clearly defined and interface contracts are enforced.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution IM is the correct owner of the availability KPI and the system-level coordination logic. Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services IMs will implement sub-strategies (redundancy, modular design, health reporting) but will not own the top-level availability calculation. This separation of concerns is clean and reduces diffusion.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of CR-NGHE-004 is feasible if NGHE Solution IM includes explicit availability tracking infrastructure. Verification approach: (1) Instrument all subsystems to report health status via Telemetry interface. (2) Implement availability calculation engine in NGHE Solution IM. (3) Run system in field for agreed evaluation period (e.g., 90 days). (4) Calculate availability ratio at end of period. (5) Audit logs to confirm measurement methodology. Feasibility is HIGH if telemetry infrastructure is reliable; MEDIUM if telemetry is unreliable.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: CR-NGHE-004 is allocated to NGHE Solution IM, which owns the measurement and reporting. Sub-requirements (FR-AVAIL-001 through FR-AVAIL-004) will be allocated to Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services, and NGHE Solution in the next decomposition level. Allocation is traceable and auditable.

recommended_revisions: Explicitly define the Telemetry interface schema to include all health data required for availability calculation (timestamp, subsystem ID, health status, fault code, event type).
Clarify ownership of failover logic: local failover (Vehicle) vs. system-level failover coordination (NGHE Solution).
Define the evaluation period and measurement methodology in the customer contract before system deployment.
Establish a baseline for availability in early field trials (Phase 1) to inform redundancy requirements in Phase 2.

decision.justification: CR-NGHE-004 is a high-level KPI that is not atomic and requires decomposition into lower-level functional requirements. The refined FR and selected DP are sound, and the IM assignment to NGHE Solution is correct. However, the sub-FRs (FR-AVAIL-001 through FR-AVAIL-004) must be decomposed further to define specific design parameters and implementation modules for health monitoring, redundancy, modular design, and reporting. Proceeding to the next decomposition level is necessary to make the requirement actionable and verifiable.
decision.justification_detail: The FR-DP-IM gates all pass, indicating that the current level is well-formed. However, the sub-FRs are still at a high level of abstraction and require further refinement. For example, FR-AVAIL-002 ('minimize unplanned downtime through predictive maintenance') needs to be decomposed into specific DPs such as 'implement condition-based monitoring with threshold-based alerts' or 'implement machine learning-based anomaly detection.' Similarly, FR-AVAIL-003 ('provide real-time availability status visibility') needs to be decomposed into DPs for dashboard design, update frequency, and alert mechanisms.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-AVAIL-001: The system shall detect and log all transitions to non-available states (faults, maintenance, safety holds) with timestamp and root cause.
FR-AVAIL-002: The system shall minimize unplanned downtime through predictive maintenance and fault isolation.
FR-AVAIL-003: The system shall provide real-time availability status visibility to Site Manager and Tele-operator.
FR-AVAIL-004: The system shall distinguish between safety-driven unavailability and performance-driven unavailability in reporting.

assumptions: Customer evaluation period is ≥30 days; shorter periods may not provide statistically significant data.
Planned maintenance windows are pre-scheduled and excluded from availability calculation.
Telemetry infrastructure (sensors, network) is reliable enough to support continuous health monitoring.
Safety-driven shutdowns are acceptable and will be logged separately; they do not invalidate the availability target.
Redundancy is cost-justified for safety-critical subsystems (steering, braking, emergency stop) but not for all subsystems.
Predictive diagnostics will improve over time as historical failure data accumulates.
open_risks: Telemetry infrastructure reliability: if sensors or network links fail, availability calculation will be inaccurate.
Predictive diagnostics accuracy: early in product lifecycle, sparse failure data may reduce prediction accuracy.
Redundancy complexity: failover logic must be thoroughly tested to avoid introducing new failure modes.
Safety-driven shutdowns: if safety events are frequent, the 0.97 target may not be achievable without addressing root safety issues.
Customer expectations: customer may expect 0.97 availability to mean 'zero safety-driven shutdowns,' which is unrealistic. Clarification is needed.
evidence_needed: Customer contract specifying evaluation period, measurement methodology, and treatment of safety-driven shutdowns.
Telemetry interface specification (schema, update frequency, reliability requirements).
Redundancy design specification (which subsystems are redundant, failover logic, testing plan).
Predictive diagnostics specification (algorithms, training data requirements, accuracy targets).
Modular design specification (component boundaries, interface contracts, replacement procedures).
Field trial data from Phase 1 (MVP) to validate availability assumptions and inform Phase 2 redundancy requirements.
